**North West Norfolk** is a [constituency](/wiki/List_of_United_Kingdom_Parliament_constituencies "List of United Kingdom Parliament constituencies")[[n 1]](#cite_note-2) represented in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") of the [UK Parliament](/wiki/UK_Parliament "UK Parliament") since 2019 by [James Wild](/wiki/James_Wild_(politician) "James Wild (politician)"), a [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)").[[n 2]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_West_Norfolk&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Under the [Redistribution of Seats Act 1885](/wiki/Redistribution_of_Seats_Act_1885 "Redistribution of Seats Act 1885"), the three two-member county divisions of Norfolk were replaced with six single-member divisions, including the newly created **North-Western Division of Norfolk**, largely formed from northern parts of the abolished [Western Division](/wiki/West_Norfolk_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "West Norfolk (UK Parliament constituency)"). It was abolished at the next redistribution of seats under the provisions of the [Representation of the People Act 1918](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1918 "Representation of the People Act 1918"), when it was largely absorbed by the expanded constituency of King's Lynn. It was re-established for the [February 1974 general election](/wiki/February_1974_United_Kingdom_general_election "February 1974 United Kingdom general election"), replacing the abolished [King's Lynn constituency](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "King's Lynn (UK Parliament constituency)").

The present constituency includes two former [Parliamentary Boroughs](/wiki/Parliamentary_boroughs "Parliamentary boroughs"), those of [Castle Rising](/wiki/Castle_Rising_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Castle Rising (UK Parliament constituency)"), which was abolished as a '[rotten borough](/wiki/Rotten_borough "Rotten borough")' in 1832, and [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "King's Lynn (UK Parliament constituency)"), abolished in 1918.

The first MP in the re-established constituency was [Christopher Brocklebank-Fowler](/wiki/Christopher_Brocklebank-Fowler "Christopher Brocklebank-Fowler"), who had gained King's Lynn, largely a [bellwether](/wiki/Bellwether_(politics) "Bellwether (politics)") seat, from one of [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson")'s government colleagues in the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"). He therefore effectively held the seat in the two 1974 elections, and in 1979; however, by March 1981, he became distanced from the [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") and defected to the newly formed [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_(UK) "Social Democratic Party (UK)") shortly before the [1983 Conservative landslide](/wiki/1983_United_Kingdom_general_election "1983 United Kingdom general election"), in which Brocklebank-Fowler lost his seat to the replacement Conservative candidate [Henry Bellingham](/wiki/Henry_Bellingham "Henry Bellingham").

Bellingham increased his precarious lead over Brocklebank-Fowler at the [1987 general election](/wiki/1987_United_Kingdom_general_election "1987 United Kingdom general election"). Therefore, at the [following election](/wiki/1992_United_Kingdom_general_election "1992 United Kingdom general election"), Brocklebank-Fowler chose to contest another seat[[n 3]](#cite_note-4) and [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)")'s candidate regained second place in this constituency, almost doubling their share of the vote. Labour gained the seat at the [1997 general election](/wiki/1997_United_Kingdom_general_election "1997 United Kingdom general election"); however, Bellingham regained the seat at the [2001 general election](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_general_election "2001 United Kingdom general election") and subsequently increased his majority in both [2005](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election") and [2010](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election").

The [2010](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election") election saw [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party") infighting when the Labour candidate for North West Norfolk, Manish Sood[[n 4]](#cite_note-5)[[2]](#cite_note-6) stated in an interview with the local newspaper *[Lynn News](/wiki/Lynn_News "Lynn News")* that [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") was "the worst prime minister we have had in this country". This gained national attention and resulted in Labour disowning their candidate. Sood did not attend the count and stated he would watch it from his home in Leicester.[[3]](#cite_note-7) He ended up finishing third, behind Bellingham and the [Liberal Democrat](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") candidate William Summers, whose party received their best ever result in the constituency,[[4]](#cite_note-8) with an 18.3% [swing](/wiki/Swing_(politics) "Swing (politics)") from Labour to the others.[[5]](#cite_note-9)[[6]](#cite_note-10)[[7]](#cite_note-11) Labour's share of the vote fell from a winning 43.8% in the 1997 election to just 13.3% in 2010, marking the steepest decline from the start to end of the thirteen years of [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") government.

Bellingham's majority fell slightly in [2015](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election"), but he retained the seat in the [2017 general election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election") with 60% of the vote, having been knighted in the New Year's [honours list](/wiki/Honours_list "Honours list") of 2016. He did not stand at the [2019 election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election") and was succeeded as the Conservative candidate by [James Wild](/wiki/James_Wild_(politician) "James Wild (politician)") who won the seat with a record majority of 42.7%.

Wild retained the seat at the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election") with a much reduced majority of 11.1% - surviving a swing of 16.2% to Labour.

## Constituency profile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_West_Norfolk&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Constituency profile")]

The constituency covers an extensive hinterland in the far corner of East Anglia - remote from [London](/wiki/London "London"), but close to Lincolnshire and the East Midlands, with which the area shares more economic links.

A minority of King's Lynn contain severe poverty marked by unemployment,[[8]](#cite_note-12) [social housing](/wiki/Social_housing "Social housing") dependency and social problems - within relatively affluent [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia"), only [Jaywick](/wiki/Jaywick "Jaywick") and [Great Yarmouth](/wiki/Great_Yarmouth "Great Yarmouth") from 2001 to 2004 scored higher in [deprivation indices](/wiki/Index_of_Multiple_Deprivation "Index of Multiple Deprivation"). Contrasted with this is the bulk of the area: the tourist resort [Hunstanton](/wiki/Hunstanton "Hunstanton"), retail, military, public sector and commercial activity of King's Lynn and the royal estate at [Sandringham](/wiki/Sandringham,_Norfolk "Sandringham, Norfolk"), along with many small villages and more than 50% undulating cultivated farmland — incomes and types of dwelling are close to the national average.

## Boundaries and boundary changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_West_Norfolk&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Boundaries and boundary changes")]

### 1885–1918

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_West_Norfolk&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1885–1918")]

* The Municipal Borough of King's Lynn; and

* The Sessional Divisions of Brothercross, Freebridge Lynn, Freebridge Marshall, and Gallow and Smithdon.[[9]](#cite_note-13)

*As [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "King's Lynn (UK Parliament constituency)") formed a separate [Parliamentary Borough](/wiki/Parliamentary_Borough "Parliamentary Borough"), only non-resident freeholders of the Borough were entitled to vote in this constituency.*

On abolition, the bulk of the Division was amalgamated with the abolished Parliamentary Borough of King's Lynn to form the new King's Lynn Division of Norfolk. Eastern areas, including [Fakenham](/wiki/Fakenham "Fakenham"), were transferred to the [Northern Division](/wiki/North_Norfolk_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "North Norfolk (UK Parliament constituency)").

### 1974–1983

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_West_Norfolk&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1974–1983")]

* The Municipal Borough of King's Lynn;
* The Urban Districts of Hunstanton and Wells-next-the-Sea; and
* The Rural Districts of Docking, Freebridge Lynn, Marshland, and Walsingham.[[10]](#cite_note-14)

*The re-established constituency was formed from the abolished constituency of King's Lynn with the addition of [Wells-next-the-Sea](/wiki/Wells-next-the-Sea "Wells-next-the-Sea") and the [Rural District of Walsingham](/wiki/Walsingham_Rural_District "Walsingham Rural District"), which included Fakenham, transferred from [North Norfolk](/wiki/North_Norfolk_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "North Norfolk (UK Parliament constituency)"). (This area is currently in the constituencies of North Norfolk and [Broadland](/wiki/Broadland_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Broadland (UK Parliament constituency)").)*

### 1983–2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_West_Norfolk&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1983–2010")]

* The Borough of [King's Lynn and West Norfolk](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn_and_West_Norfolk "King's Lynn and West Norfolk") wards of Burnham, Chase, Clenchwarton, Creake, Dersingham, Docking, Gayton, Gaywood Central, Gaywood North, Gaywood South, Grimston, Heacham, Hunstanton, Lynn Central, Lynn North, Lynn South West, Mershe Lande, Middleton, North Coast, Priory, Rudham, St Lawrence, St Margaret's, Snettisham, Spellowfields, The Walpoles, The Woottons, Valley Hill, West Walton, West Winch, and Wiggenhall.[[11]](#cite_note-15)[[12]](#cite_note-16)

*Wells-next-the-Sea and areas comprising the former Rural District of Walsingham, including Fakenham, were transferred back to North Norfolk. Minor realignment of the boundary with [South West Norfolk](/wiki/South_West_Norfolk "South West Norfolk").*

![Map](https://maps.wikimedia.org/img/osm-intl,a,a,a,300x200.png?lang=en&domain=en.wikipedia.org&title=North_West_Norfolk&revid=1345286291&groups=_8817984aea9028cb42ca60635f29b3e3bec73384&parser=legacy)

Map of boundaries 2010–2024

### 2010–2024

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_West_Norfolk&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2010–2024")]

* The Borough of King's Lynn and West Norfolk wards of Brancaster, Burnham, Clenchwarton, Dersingham, Docking, Fairstead, Gayton, Gaywood Chase, Gaywood North Bank, Grimston, Heacham, Hunstanton, North Lynn, North Wootton, Old Gaywood, Priory, Rudham, St Margaret's with St Nicholas, Snettisham, South and West Lynn, South Wootton, Spellowfields, Springwood, Valley Hill, Walpole, and West Winch.[[13]](#cite_note-17)

*Small area transferred to South West Norfolk.*

### Current

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_West_Norfolk&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Current")]

Further to the [2023 Periodic Review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/2023_Periodic_Review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2023 Periodic Review of Westminster constituencies"), which came into effect for the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election"), the composition of the constituency is as follows (as they existed on 1 December 2020):

* The Borough of King's Lynn and West Norfolk wards of: Bircham with Rudhams; Brancaster; Burnham Market & Docking; Clenchwarton; Dersingham; Fairstead; Gayton & Grimston; Gaywood Chase; Gaywood Clock; Gaywood North Bank; Heacham; Hunstanton; Massingham with Castle Acre; North Lynn; St. Margaret’s with St. Nicholas; Snettisham; South & West Lynn; Springwood; Terrington; The Woottons; Walsoken, West Walton & Walpole; West Winch.[[14]](#cite_note-18)

*Minor net gain from [South West Norfolk](/wiki/South_West_Norfolk "South West Norfolk") following changes to local authority ward boundaries.*
